title: Standard reduction potential E°
definition: A **standard reduction potential** $E^\circ$ is the voltage a reduction half-reaction ($\mathrm{M}^{n+} + n\,e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{M}$) drives at standard conditions, measured against the **standard hydrogen electrode** (SHE), which is *defined* as exactly 0 V. A **more positive** $E^\circ$ means a stronger pull on electrons — that couple is reduced (it is the **cathode**); the couple with the lower $E^\circ$ is forced to run backwards as oxidation (the **anode**). Only **differences** are physical: the zero point (the SHE) is an arbitrary reference, so a single $E^\circ$ is meaningful only relative to another. The values are a sourced, tabulated measured dataset (regime-3); the cell voltage built from them, $E^\circ_\text{cell} = E^\circ_\text{cathode} - E^\circ_\text{anode}$, is exact given the table (see [[cell-potential]]).
